description: Biography requires a documented subject, real relationships, archival artifacts, and the tension between public persona and private self — hagiography is the primary anti-pattern.

character_constraints.description: A single documented person whose life is the narrative subject — rendered with complexity, not idealization.
Family members, rivals, collaborators, or mentors documented in the historical record.
The gap between public persona and private self — a journalist, biographer, or public who sees only the surface.

relationship_constraints.description: The tension between who the subject is publicly and who they are privately — this gap is the narrative engine.
A formative relationship that shaped the subject's trajectory — documented, not invented.

place_constraints.description: Real places rendered with period accuracy — the subject's actual environments.
Where the subject is unguarded — home, studio, sickbed — the site of private truth.
The stage, podium, courtroom, or arena where the public persona operates.

object_constraints.description: Letters, notebooks, photographs, recordings — primary sources that anchor the narrative in documented reality.
An object that carries the weight of the subject's inner life — instrument, manuscript, keepsake.

element_rules.description: A defining moment scene and a crucible scene (maximum pressure) are both mandatory — the biography must dramatize turning points.
element_rules.testable_condition: The timeline contains at least one 'defines' transition and one 'endures' or 'breaks under' transition.

element_rules.description: Hagiography — suppressing failures, flaws, and contradictions — is the primary anti-pattern and must be absent.
element_rules.testable_condition: The timeline includes at least one scene depicting the subject's failure, flaw, or moral compromise.
